serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter for 2–3 persons, estimated at approximately 900 g total (including meat, potato wedges, and garden salad), yielding roughly 300–450 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate for mixed grilled meats (beef sirloin, chicken thigh, pork ribs) with potato wedges and garden salad
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact multi-protein Italian grilled platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of the five named components using USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries: (1) grilled beef sirloin (FDC ID 174032), (2) grilled chicken thigh, skinless (FDC ID 171477), (3) grilled pork ribs (FDC ID 167903), (4) baked/oven potato wedges (FDC ID 170030), (5) garden salad mixed greens (FDC ID 172065). Assumed platter composition by weight: beef sirloin 30%, chicken thigh 25%, pork ribs 25%, potato wedges 15%, garden salad 5%. All values expressed per 100 g of combined platter weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Italian mixed grilled meat platter. GI estimated as a weighted average of component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021: grilled beef/chicken/pork (GI ~0, protein-dominant, negligible glycemic response), potato wedges (GI ~65 for baked/oven potato), garden salad (GI ~15). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution: potato wedges dominate the carbohydrate load (~78% of total carbs), salad contributes ~10%, meat contributes ~12% (from glycogen/trace). Composite GI estimated at approximately 45 (low), reflecting that the majority of carbohydrate comes from potato wedges moderated by the large protein and fat content of the platter which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 39.7 g, where carbohydrate per 900 g serving = 9.8 × 9 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full individual portion (~300–450 g) may contribute 930–1395 mg sodium, representing 40–60% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning and marinades, limit portion size to one-third of the platter, and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or steak sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite GI estimated at 45 (low), but glycemic load per individual serving (~300 g) is approximately 13 g, driven primarily by potato wedges; the high protein and fat content moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include this platter in moderation, prioritising the grilled meats and salad while limiting potato wedge intake to reduce glycemic load; pairing with non-starchy vegetables further attenuates postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.4 g per 100 g from mixed animal proteins (Angus beef, Iberico pork ribs); an individual portion contributes approximately 10–15 g saturated fat, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g for adults.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, favour the chicken thigh and salad components over the beef sirloin and pork ribs, and avoid high-fat sauces; the lean protein content is beneficial but saturated fat from red meat and pork should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grigliata Mista, meaning 'mixed grill' in Italian, is a substantial sharing platter of assorted grilled meats originating from Italian culinary tradition and served at Italian restaurants in Singapore. This version, as offered at Ricciotti – The Riverwalk on field survey data, comprises four protein components — Premium Angus beef sirloin, Diavola-style boneless chicken thighs (marinated with chilli and spices), Iberico pork ribs, and accompaniments of potato wedges and a garden salad. The meats are cooked over a grill or chargrill, producing characteristic smoky, caramelised exterior surfaces with juicy interiors. The flavour profile is savoury, smoky, and rich, with the Iberico pork ribs contributing a distinctive nutty sweetness from the acorn-fed breed. Nutritionally, the platter is high in protein (~18.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~10.2 g per 100 g, of which ~3.4 g is saturated), and relatively low in carbohydrates (~9.8 g per 100 g), with the carbohydrate load concentrated in the potato wedges. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), moderated by the dominant protein and fat content. Sodium is a clinical consideration at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to marinades and seasoning. The dish is suitable for sharing between 2–3 persons and is positioned as a premium restaurant experience. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those observing halal dietary requirements without confirmation of halal certification. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (potato wedge glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from red meat and pork ribs).

What meats are in an Italian mixed grill?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Italian mixed grilled meat platter as served at Ricciotti Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of five USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods / SR Legacy component entries using a platter weight distribution assumption (beef 30%, chicken 25%, pork 25%, potato 15%, salad 5%). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives', as the dish is predominantly a multi-protein grilled meat platter. 2. Nutrient values are weighted-average estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no exact match exists in any approved primary source for this composite dish. 3. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this platter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed; Ricciotti is an Italian restaurant and pork is present (Iberico pork ribs), so is_halal is set to false. 5. Allergen information is not fully determinable from available data; restaurant-specific allergen declarations should be consulted. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not independently verified for this exact dish. 7. This is an Italian cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules. 8. Price SGD 139.52 is preserved from input as a contextual data point only and does not affect nutritional scoring.
